text: The robotic instruments retract the right vas deferens and isolate the external iliac vein within the pelvis, providing a clear view of the prostate and surrounding anatomy in preparation for lymph node dissection.

The surgeon dissects the external iliac vein on the left side, ensuring exposure for the upcoming lymph node dissection. The dissection proceeds to the endopelvic fascia, isolating the puboprostatic ligaments and removing fat from the plane to reveal the prostate anatomy.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to fulgurate the superficial dorsal venous complex, a vein associated with the prostate. Concurrently, the periprostatic or anterior pelvic fat is being removed to enhance exposure of the prostate, with the excised fat sent for analysis to check for lymph node involvement.

The surgeon exposes the endopelvic fascia, incising it to dissect the prostate from surrounding pelvic muscles. Electrocautery is used to cut the puboprostatic ligament, which attaches the prostate to the underside of the pubic bone. The same anatomical structures are visible on the left side, where the puboprostatic ligament is also being brought down.

The surgeon isolates the dorsal venous complex and ligates it using sutures. This large vein, which transports blood away from the prostate, is carefully controlled to prevent significant bleeding during the robotic prostatectomy.

The surgeon dissects between the bladder neck and the prostate, using the catheter balloon as a visual landmark to delineate the space. The catheter is visualized and brought into the surgical field, secured with the robotic fourth arm, while the assistant pulls on the catheter externally to enhance exposure.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection between the back of the prostate and the bladder, progressing down to the vas deferens and seminal vesicles. Care is taken to remove cancerous tissue while preserving the bladder's posterior wall and surrounding nerves crucial for erection and continence.

The surgeon secures the vas deferens with an absorbable clip, repeating the process on the opposite side to elevate the seminal vesicles and vas deferens behind the prostate. Blood vessels to the prostate are clipped and dissected, followed by the posterior dissection that separates the rectum from the prostate. The deep dorsal vein complex is then incised.
The surgeon maintains the suspension suture in place, visibly suspending the urethra or bladder tube to the inside of the pubic bone, which is intended to reduce leakage following catheter removal.

The surgeon performs nerve-sparing dissection of the neurovascular bundle on the right side of the prostate, carefully separating the bundle from the prostate while minimizing the use of heat to reduce trauma to the nerves.
The surgeon clips and cuts the blood vessels of the prostatic pedicle, completing the separation of the neurovascular bundle from the prostate on the right side.

The surgeon performs urethral dissection at the apex of the prostate, carefully preserving as much urethra as possible to maintain urinary continence while ensuring complete removal of prostate cancer. The dissection is delicate due to the high risk of positive margins in this area.

The surgeon performs a pelvic lymph node dissection on both sides, carefully avoiding the obturator nerve. The lymph nodes, prostate, seminal vesicles, and vas deferens are placed into a specimen bag for diagnostic and therapeutic purposes.

The surgeon applies a hemostatic agent along the neurovascular bundles to reduce bleeding. The assistant manipulates the catheter externally to guide the needle placement internally for the anastomosis. The posterior plate of the anastomosis is completed, and the suture is brought around to finalize the connection between the bladder neck and urethra.
